alsoKnownAs: Peacock Moss (trade overlap; some retailers treat Peacock and Spiky as the same Taxiphyllum cultivar, others as distinct)

nativeRange.habitat: Originates from Southeast Asia, where Taxiphyllum mosses grow attached to logs and rocks along the margins of ponds and streams in humid riparian zones

decorPreferences: driftwood (spiky triangular fronds showcase well on branching wood)
stainless or plastic mesh for moss walls and carpets
tied with thread/fishing line or attached with cyanoacrylate gel until rhizoids anchor
gentle to moderate flow keeps debris from settling between the spiky fronds

summary: Spiky Moss is a Taxiphyllum cultivar prized for its distinctive upward-pointing, triangle-shaped fronds that give it a denser, more architectural silhouette than Java Moss. It is best attached to driftwood, rock, or mesh rather than buried in substrate, and tolerates a wide range of light and water conditions.
careNotes:
Spiky Moss (Taxiphyllum sp. 'Spiky') is one of several aquarium cultivars in the genus Taxiphyllum, alongside Java Moss ([fw-plant-033]), Flame Moss ([fw-plant-132]), and others. It is not currently tied to a formally described species — it sits as an undescribed or uncertain Taxiphyllum related to T. barbieri — but it is well established in the trade for its distinctive triangle-shaped, upward-pointing fronds that form a denser, more architecturally defined mat than Java Moss's loosely bushy growth.

PEACOCK MOSS OVERLAP: There is genuine disagreement in the hobby about whether Spiky Moss and Peacock Moss are the same Taxiphyllum cultivar expressing differently under varying conditions — one position holds that low light produces longer, looser growth while high light with CO2 produces denser carpeting — or whether the two are truly distinct cultivars. Many retailers list them separately. Shoppers should be aware that stock sold as 'Peacock Moss' may or may not match what is sold as 'Spiky Moss' at another store.

ATTACHMENT: Spiky Moss is an epiphyte, meaning it grows anchored to a surface rather than rooted in soil. Tie or glue it to driftwood, porous rock, lava rock, or plastic or stainless-steel mesh; do NOT bury it in substrate. Small root-like structures called rhizoids anchor the moss to the surface over several weeks, after which any thread or fishing line can be removed if desired.

Lighting tolerance is broad — the moss is generally considered to grow well even under low light, and moderate light tends to produce denser, more compact, and more uniformly spiked fronds. CO2 supplementation is optional; the plant grows without it, but supplementation tends to produce denser, faster growth. Fertilization demand is low: the moss absorbs nutrients directly from the water column and does not require a nutrient-rich substrate.

SHRIMP AND FRY REFUGE: Dense mats of Spiky Moss make excellent microhabitat for newly hatched fry and dwarf-shrimp larvae, which graze biofilm and microfauna from the fronds.
breedingNotes: In aquarium conditions, Spiky Moss reproduces entirely by vegetative propagation: any healthy portion cut or pulled from the mat will regrow when re-attached to a new surface, with no need to wait for a reproductive cycle. True sexual reproduction — spore production from a sporophyte (the spore-bearing stage of the moss life cycle) — requires the plant to grow partially or fully out of water and is essentially never observed in submerged aquarium tanks.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Taxiphyllum sp. 'Spiky Moss'
primary.notes: Primary retailer product page. Used for: scientific name (Taxiphyllum sp. 'Spiky Moss'), beginner care level ('very undemanding'), low-light tolerance ('grows well even in very low light conditions'), propagation by breaking off clumps and relocating, epiphyte attachment to hardscape, distinguishing 'spiky deep green branches that extend out in multiple directions' versus Christmas Moss.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Spiky Moss Ultimate Care Guide
additional.notes: Primary numeric-parameter source. Used for: temperature 22–28 °C (72–82 °F), pH 6.5–7.5, gH 4–12 dGH, kH 1–8 dKH, low-to-medium lighting (excess light encourages algae), CO2 optional (supplementation yields denser/faster growth), Southeast Asian origin, epiphyte attachment to hardscape, shrimp/fry refuge utility.

additional.name: Fluvico — Peacock Moss / Spiky Moss Care Guide
additional.notes: Second cross-verification source. Used for: temperature 22–28 °C, pH 5.5–7.5 (body) / 6.0–7.5 (headline), low-to-medium lighting, CO2 optional, slow growth rate, beginner difficulty, ~10 cm size, asserts that Peacock Moss and Spiky Moss are 'the exact same moss' with appearance varying by light/CO2 (basis for the alsoKnownAs entry and the careNotes 'Peacock Moss overlap' paragraph).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Taxiphyllum
additional.notes: Genus-level taxonomic authority (no species page exists for the 'Spiky' cultivar). Used for: family Hypnaceae, order Hypnales, class Bryopsida, ~44 species in genus, mat-forming creeping growth habit, broad continental distribution including Asia. Confirms that 'Spiky' is a trade cultivar not formally described as a separate Taxiphyllum species in the Wikipedia genus list.

propagation: drape over branching driftwood to showcase upward-pointing spiky fronds
